title: nand2tetris — The Elements of Computing Systems
summary: A complete bottom-up construction of a general-purpose 16-bit computer starting from a single Nand gate primitive. The project spans five progressive layers: combinational Boolean logic, binary arithmetic, clocked sequential memory, Hack assembly language programming, and the full computer architecture integrating CPU, memory, and instruction ROM.
instructionPreview: This project follows the classic from-Nand-to-general-purpose-computer curriculum. You must build a complete general-purpose computer from the ground up, starting from Nand gates and progressing through Boolean logic, arithmetic, sequential memory, machine language programming, and computer architecture. All source files with `// TODO: implement` stubs must be completed.
instruction:
This project follows the classic from-Nand-to-general-purpose-computer curriculum. You must build a complete general-purpose computer from the ground up, starting from Nand gates and progressing through Boolean logic, arithmetic, sequential memory, machine language programming, and computer architecture. All source files with `// TODO: implement` stubs must be completed.

Your working directory is `/workspace`. The project code is already present. You need to implement all the modules and features listed below so that all tests pass.

## Modules to Implement

- **Boolean Logic** (`projects/01/`): Implement all 15 combinational logic chips using only the built-in `Nand` gate as the base primitive. Chips include 1-bit gates (Not, And, Or, Xor), a multiplexer (Mux), a demultiplexer (DMux), 16-bit variants of each gate, an 8-input Or, and multi-way multiplexers/demultiplexers of various widths. (~15 HDL files)

- **Boolean Arithmetic** (`projects/02/`): Implement adder circuits and an Arithmetic Logic Unit. The ALU accepts two 16-bit inputs and six control bits (zx, nx, zy, ny, f, no) and produces a 16-bit output along with two status flags (zr for zero, ng for negative). (~5 HDL files)

- **Sequential Logic** (`projects/03/a/` and `projects/03/b/`): Implement clocked memory elements building from a 1-bit register up through a hierarchy of RAM chips. The `DFF` (D flip-flop) is available as a built-in primitive for sequential circuits. Chips include a 1-bit register, 16-bit register, and RAM modules from 8 words up through RAM512, RAM4K, and RAM16K. The 16-bit Program Counter supports increment, load, and reset operations. (~8 HDL files)

- **Machine Language** (`projects/04/`, plus canonical Hack assembly examples in `projects/06/add/`, `projects/06/max/`, and `projects/06/rect/`): Write `Mult.asm` and `Fill.asm`, then restore the small sample programs `Add.asm`, `Max.asm`, `MaxL.asm`, `Rect.asm`, and `RectL.asm`. Together these files exercise repeated addition, screen-filling loops, branching, symbolic labels, and raw numeric addressing in the Hack instruction set. (~7 ASM files)

- **Computer Architecture** (`projects/05/`): Implement the complete Hack computer by wiring together all previously built chips. `Memory.hdl` unifies 16K RAM, an 8K screen memory map, and a keyboard register into a single address space. `CPU.hdl` decodes and executes Hack machine instructions using the ALU, A register, D register, and program counter. `Computer.hdl` connects the CPU, Memory, and a ROM32K into the top-level computer. (~3 HDL files)

## HDL Reference

HDL syntax: `CHIP Name { IN pins; OUT pins; PARTS: ChipName(pin=wire, ...); ... }`
Bus slices: `a[0]`, `a[0..7]`, `false`, `true` are valid wire values. Built-in chips available: `Nand`, `DFF`, `ARegister`, `DRegister`, `ROM32K`, `Screen`, `Keyboard`, `RAM16K` (only for Memory.hdl — you must implement your own RAM16K in projects/03/).

## Hack Assembly Reference

A-instruction: `@value` — loads a 15-bit value into the A register.
C-instruction: `dest=comp;jump` — computes, optionally stores result, optionally jumps.
Labels: `(LABEL)` defines a ROM address symbol. Predefined symbols: R0-R15, SP, LCL, ARG, THIS, THAT, SCREEN (16384), KBD (24576).

moduleDag.project: nand2tetris — The Elements of Computing Systems
moduleDag.description: A complete bottom-up construction of a general-purpose 16-bit computer starting from a single Nand gate primitive. The project spans five progressive layers: combinational Boolean logic, binary arithmetic, clocked sequential memory, Hack assembly language programming, and the full computer architecture integrating CPU, memory, and instruction ROM.

nodes.label: Boolean Logic (Project 01)
nodes.description: Fifteen combinational chips built exclusively from the built-in Nand gate. Covers 1-bit elementary gates (Not, And, Or, Xor), a multiplexer (Mux) and demultiplexer (DMux), 16-bit bus-wide variants of each, an 8-input Or reduction (Or8Way), and multi-way multiplexers/demultiplexers (Mux4Way16, Mux8Way16, DMux4Way, DMux8Way). These chips are the universal primitive layer on which all higher-level chips are built.

nodes.label: Hack Assembly Programs (Projects 04 and 06 examples)
nodes.description: Hack assembly programs tested by a pre-built CPU emulator (no HDL dependency). Mult.asm multiplies R0×R1 via repeated addition into R2. Fill.asm polls the keyboard memory map and blackens or clears the entire screen memory map in response to key presses, running indefinitely. The same unit also restores the small project06 sample programs Add.asm, Max.asm, MaxL.asm, Rect.asm, and RectL.asm, covering straight-line arithmetic, branching, symbolic labels, and raw numeric addressing in the Hack instruction set.

nodes.label: Boolean Arithmetic (Project 02)
nodes.description: Adder circuits and the ALU built on top of Boolean Logic chips. HalfAdder and FullAdder compose into the 16-bit ripple-carry Add16; Inc16 wraps Add16 for increment. The ALU accepts two 16-bit operands and six control bits (zx, nx, zy, ny, f, no) to select among 18 arithmetic and bitwise operations, producing a 16-bit result plus zero (zr) and negative (ng) status flags.

nodes.label: Sequential Logic — Small RAM (Project 03a)
nodes.description: Clocked memory elements built from the built-in DFF primitive plus chips from Boolean Logic and Boolean Arithmetic. Bit is a 1-bit load-enabled register; Register composes sixteen Bit cells; RAM8 and RAM64 use DMux8Way/Mux8Way16 for address decoding. PC uses Inc16 from boolean_arithmetic and Register to support increment, synchronous load, and synchronous reset operations.

nodes.label: Sequential Logic — Large RAM (Project 03b)
nodes.description: Larger RAM modules assembled hierarchically from the smaller chips of sequential_logic_small. RAM512 arrays eight RAM64 banks with a 9-bit address; RAM4K arrays eight RAM512 banks with a 12-bit address; RAM16K uses DMux4Way and Mux4Way16 (from boolean_logic) to bank four RAM4K chips with a 14-bit address, forming the main data memory for the Hack computer.

nodes.label: Computer Architecture (Project 05)
nodes.description: The complete Hack computer assembled from all prior components. Memory.hdl unifies RAM16K (sequential_logic_large), the Screen memory map, and Keyboard register into a single 24K+1 address space via DMux4Way and Mux4Way16. CPU.hdl decodes A- and C-instructions, routes operands through the ALU (boolean_arithmetic), updates A/D registers, and computes the next PC value (sequential_logic_small) via jump logic. Computer.hdl is the top-level chip wiring CPU, Memory, and ROM32K so the PC drives the ROM address.

edges.label: HalfAdder uses Xor, And; FullAdder uses Or; ALU uses Mux16, Not16, And16, Or8Way, Or, Not from boolean_logic
Bit uses Mux; RAM8 and RAM64 use DMux8Way and Mux8Way16 from boolean_logic
PC uses Inc16 from boolean_arithmetic for the increment operation
RAM512 builds on RAM64 (sequential_logic_small); RAM4K on RAM512; RAM16K on RAM4K
Memory uses DMux4Way, Mux4Way16; CPU uses Mux16, Not, And, Or directly from boolean_logic
CPU.hdl instantiates the ALU built in boolean_arithmetic
CPU.hdl instantiates PC built in sequential_logic_small
Memory.hdl instantiates RAM16K built in sequential_logic_large
